critic_raw:
The hybrid CNN-RNN design for hospital readmission prediction makes a reasonable effort at a clean experimental setup, but the comparison is fundamentally flawed: the CNN-RNN model has access to raw temporal sequences of treatments and medications, while the logistic regression baseline works on engineered features excluding those sequences. The design explicitly acknowledges this ('logistic regression cannot process raw sequences natively'), but this is not a methodologically acceptable justification. The claim being tested is about the value of the hybrid architecture, not just the value of raw sequences. A fair comparison requires at minimum a sequential logistic regression alternative on handcrafted temporal features, or a comparison against a simpler RNN (e.g., LSTM alone without CNN) to isolate what the convolutional component contributes.

The handling of missing data is methodologically weak for a clinical EHR study. Records with more than 20% missing values are simply excluded. In clinical datasets, missingness is systematically related to patient acuity — patients who are too sick to have complete records are disproportionately excluded, introducing selection bias that inflates performance metrics on the cleaner subset. The design does not characterize the excluded population or test whether exclusion changes the class balance or severity distribution.

AUPRC is chosen as the primary metric, which is appropriate for imbalanced classification. However, the design also mentions using the F1-score as a secondary metric without specifying the threshold at which F1 is evaluated. F1 is threshold-dependent; unless the threshold is fixed by a clinically meaningful criterion independent of the test set, this introduces post-hoc threshold selection bias. The design states that hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set but does not specify whether threshold selection also uses validation, and whether this threshold is locked before test set evaluation.

The department-level readmission baseline rate is included as a feature in both models (computed from training data), which is a reasonable design choice. However, for small departments (n < 500), this feature will be a highly noisy estimate that could hurt the logistic regression more than the CNN-RNN if the neural model is more robust to noisy inputs. This differential sensitivity is not acknowledged or tested.
defender_raw:
The hybrid CNN-RNN versus logistic regression comparison for hospital readmission prediction is a sound and practically motivated experiment. The temporal split — training on January 2019–December 2021, validating on January–August 2022, testing on September 2022–December 2023 — correctly mirrors the deployment scenario where the model is trained on historical data and applied prospectively. This is not just methodological formalism; it matters because hospital coding practices, discharge criteria, and patient population characteristics evolve over time, and only a temporal split reveals whether the model generalizes to genuine distribution shift rather than just held-out data from the same period.

The stratification by hospital department within each temporal window is a thoughtful confounder control that many comparable studies omit. Department assignment is correlated with case complexity, which is itself correlated with readmission risk. Without departmental stratification, it would be possible for one split to be enriched with high-complexity admissions simply by chance, inflating or deflating performance estimates in a way unrelated to model quality. The design explicitly addresses this confound without introducing temporal leakage — a careful and defensible choice.

The data quality protocol is appropriately conservative. Excluding records with greater than 20% missing values in key fields, using training-set median imputation for remaining missing numeric values, and assigning an explicit 'missing' category for categorical variables are all standard practices that avoid test-set leakage. Critics who argue for more sophisticated imputation are introducing unnecessary complexity: for a comparative study where both models see the same imputed data, simple consistent imputation is sufficient and more reproducible. The goal is not to solve the imputation problem but to ensure the comparison between CNN-RNN and logistic regression is fair.

Using logistic regression as the baseline is the right choice for this comparison. A well-engineered logistic regression on tabular EHR features is a clinically deployed standard and represents a genuine performance bar, not a strawman. Demonstrating that a hybrid neural architecture outperforms a well-specified logistic regression on temporal clinical data would be a meaningful contribution that is neither trivially easy nor unfairly stacked.
all_issues_raised: Unfair comparison: CNN-RNN accesses raw temporal sequences while logistic regression uses only engineered features; the architectural contribution cannot be isolated
Exclusion of records with >20% missing values introduces systematic selection bias toward less-sick patients, inflating performance metrics
F1-score threshold is not fixed a priori; threshold selection without a pre-specified criterion introduces post-hoc selection bias
Small-department baseline feature is a noisy estimate that may differentially disadvantage logistic regression vs. the neural model; not tested
No ablation comparing CNN-RNN to simpler RNN or MLP; the contribution of the convolutional component is never isolated
all_issues_adjudicated: Unfair comparison: CNN-RNN accesses raw temporal sequences while logistic regression uses only engineered features; the architectural contribution cannot be isolated
Exclusion of records with >20% missing values introduces systematic selection bias toward less-sick patients, inflating performance metrics
F1-score threshold is not fixed a priori; threshold selection without a pre-specified criterion introduces post-hoc selection bias
No ablation comparing CNN-RNN to simpler RNN or MLP; the contribution of the convolutional component is never isolated
